constraints.analysis_summary: After round 1, no information has been gained because the guess was empty. All colors remain possible, and no positions are determined. The search space is the full set of all possible 4-color sequences (with repeats allowed).

Prior round (empty guess) gave no information. Combined with round 2, we now know that the secret consists of exactly 4 of the 6 guessed colors (red, blue, green, yellow, white, black), each appearing once (distinct), and none of them is in the guessed position. The two colors not in the secret are unknown. No locked positions or confirmed colors yet.

Prior round (empty) gave no info. Round2 established that secret consists of 4 distinct colors from {red, blue, green, yellow, white, black} with no correct positions. Round3 now reveals that exactly one of {yellow, white, black} is present and locked in a specific position (index 0,1, or 2 respectively). Consequently, red, blue, green are all present. Purple, orange, pink are impossible. The positions of red, blue, green must avoid their round2 guessed positions (0,1,2) and fill the remaining three positions. All three scenarios are still logically possible, so the solution space has 9 equally likely candidates.

Previous rounds: Round 2 established secret has 4 distinct colors from {red, blue, green, yellow, white, black}. Round 3 showed exactly one of {yellow, white, black} is present and locked in its guessed position. Round 4 pegs=5 forces green, red, blue, and one of {yellow, white} to be present, and rules out black. Combining all: green must be at position 1 (from its allowed positions), which conflicts with white requiring position 1, so white is impossible; thus yellow is the present trio color and must be at position 0 (from round3). Red and blue then occupy positions 2 and 3. With blue not allowed at position 2 (round4) and red allowed at both, we get blue at position 3 and red at position 2. Therefore, the secret is completely determined: [yellow, green, red, blue].

All rounds combined (secret length 6): The secret uses colors yellow, green, red, blue with multiplicities 2,2,1,1 respectively. Yellow is locked at position 0. Red cannot be at pos0, blue cannot be at pos1, green cannot be at pos2, yellow cannot be at pos3. Green cannot be at pos0 or pos3. Blue cannot be at pos2. Red cannot be at pos1. Yellow cannot be at pos4. From round5, exactly 3 positions are correct: pos0 is correct, and exactly two of {pos1, pos2, pos3} are correct. This leads to three possible sequences: (1) [Y, G, Y, B, G, R], (2) [Y, G, Y, B, R, G], (3) [Y, Y, R, B, G, G]. All satisfy all feedback from all rounds.

All rounds combined: The secret code is uniquely determined as [yellow, green, yellow, blue, green, red]. This satisfies all feedback from rounds 2-6. Round 2: 4 out of its 6 guessed colors (red, blue, green, yellow) are in secret, none in correct position (confirmed: red≠0, blue≠1, green≠2, yellow≠3). Round 3: exactly one of {yellow, white, black} present and locked at guessed position → yellow at pos0. Round 4: 5 pegs, 0 positions → green not at 0 or 3, red not at 1, blue not at 2, and confirms yellow present. Round 5: 5 pegs, 3 correct positions (pos0, pos1, pos3) → matches. Round 6: 5 pegs, 1 correct position (pos0) → matches. No further unknowns remain.

All rounds combined: The secret code is [yellow, green, yellow, blue, red, green]. This satisfies all feedback from rounds 2-7. Round 2 (4 pegs, 0 positions): red, blue, green, yellow all present, none in correct positions (verified). Round 3 (1 peg, 1 position): yellow locked at pos0. Round 4 (5 pegs, 0 positions): green appears twice, red, blue, yellow all present, none in correct positions (verified). Round 5 (5 pegs, 3 positions): correct at pos0, pos1, pos3 (yellow, green, blue) – matches. Round 6 (5 pegs, 1 position): correct only at pos0, all others wrong – matches. Round 7 (5 pegs, 4 positions): correct at pos0,1,3,4 – matches. No unknown positions remain.

All rounds combined: The secret code is [yellow, green, yellow, blue, red, green]. This round (8) validates the same solution with feedback 5 pegs, 4 positions (pos0,1,3,4 correct; green at pos2 misplaced). No contradictions or additional constraints. Confidence is maximum.

reasoning_steps: Step 1: IDENTIFY EXISTING COLORS - The guess has 6 distinct colors: red, blue, green, yellow, white, black. Feedback pegs=4 means exactly 4 of these 6 colors exist in the secret. Since the secret has 4 positions, all secret colors must be among the guessed colors (if a secret color were not in the guess, pegs would be at most 3). Therefore, the secret is a permutation of 4 distinct colors from this set. No specific colors can be confirmed yet.
Step 2: IDENTIFY LOCKED POSITIONS - No correct positions (positions=0), so no positions are locked from this round.

Step 3: IDENTIFY MISPLACED COLORS - Misplaced count = pegs - positions = 4. So the 4 colors that are in the secret are each in a wrong position relative to the guess. However, we don't know which specific colors are in the secret, so we cannot list specific misplaced colors yet.

Step 4: IDENTIFY IMPOSSIBLE COLORS - We know that exactly 2 of the 6 guessed colors are not in the secret, but we don't know which. So no specific color can be declared impossible yet. Colors not in the guess (if any) would be impossible, but since the palette is assumed to be these 6, all remain possible until further evidence.

Step 5: IDENTIFY UNKNOWNS - All 4 positions are unknown. The secret must be composed of 4 distinct colors from the set of 6, with the constraint that none of those colors appears in the same position as guessed. This leaves many possibilities (e.g., choose 4 colors and arrange them avoiding their guessed positions).

The two colors not in the secret are unknown. No locked positions or confirmed colors yet.

None of the colors placed in positions 0-5 (the guess positions) are correct; each secret color occupies a different position than its guess index.

reasoning_steps: Step 1: IDENTIFY EXISTING COLORS - The guess contains 6 colors: yellow, white, black, purple, orange, pink. Feedback pegs=1 means exactly one color from this guess is in the secret. From round2, we already know secret colors must be from {red, blue, green, yellow, white, black}. So only yellow, white, black are candidates; purple, orange, pink cannot be in secret. Thus the one existing color is exactly one of {yellow, white, black}. The other two of those three are not in secret. Combined with round2's conclusion that 4 of the 6 colors are in secret, this forces red, blue, green to all be present (since the two absent from the set are the two non-selected from {yellow, white, black}). So confirmed existing colors: red, blue, green, plus exactly one of {yellow, white, black}.

Step 2: IDENTIFY LOCKED POSITIONS - Feedback positions=1 means one color is in the exact correct position. Since the guess length is 6 but secret has only 4 positions, only indices 0–3 correspond to secret positions. The correct position must be from the color that exists. The three candidates appear at indices 0 (yellow), 1 (white), 2 (black). So either yellow at pos0, white at pos1, or black at pos2 is locked. However, we don't know which, so no specific locked position can be recorded yet.

Step 3: IDENTIFY MISPLACED COLORS - Misplaced count = pegs - positions = 0, so no misplaced colors in this round. However, from round2 we have previous constraints: red cannot be at pos0, blue at pos1, green at pos2. These are treated as misplaced constraints because those colors exist but are not in those positions.

Step 4: IDENTIFY IMPOSSIBLE COLORS - Any color from this guess with pegs=0 would be impossible if the guessed color also never appeared in any previous round with positive pegs. Here, purple, orange, pink each had pegs=0 individually (since total pegs=1 from all six). They are not in the round2 set, so they are impossible. Also, from {yellow, white, black}, two of them are impossible, but we don't know which yet. So definitely impossible: purple, orange, pink.

Step 5: IDENTIFY UNKNOWNS - We know the secret's four colors: red, blue, green, and one of {yellow, white, black}. The positions are partially constrained: the present trio color goes to one specific position (0,1, or 2) and the other three positions must be a permutation of red, blue, green subject to round2 constraints (red≠0, blue≠1, green≠2). There are 3 possible cases (yellow/white/black present) each with 3 valid permutations, so 9 possible codes. The exact positions of red, blue, green and which trio color is present remain unknown.

reasoning_steps: Step 1: IDENTIFY EXISTING COLORS - The guess has greens at pos0 and pos3, red at pos1, blue at pos2, yellow at pos4, white at pos5. pegs=5 means 5 of these 6 colors exist in the secret. Since green, red, blue are already known to be present from prior rounds, they contribute 2+1+1=4 pegs. The 5th peg must come from either yellow or white (since both greens count regardless of secret count). Therefore, exactly one of yellow or white is present in the secret. Black cannot be present because that would give only 4 pegs. So secret contains green, red, blue, and one of {yellow, white}.

Step 2: IDENTIFY LOCKED POSITIONS - positions=0 means no guess color is in the correct position. For the first 4 guess positions (only ones that map to secret): green not at pos0, red not at pos1, blue not at pos2, green not at pos3. Combined with prior constraints (red not at pos0 from round2, blue not at pos1, green not at pos2), we deduce: green can only be at pos1; red can be at pos2 or pos3; blue can be at pos0 or pos3. No locked positions from this round alone.

Step 3: IDENTIFY MISPLACED COLORS - misplaced = pegs - positions = 5. All 5 correct color occurrences are in wrong positions relative to the guess. Specifically: green at pos0 and pos3 are misplaced (correct but wrong positions), red at pos1 is misplaced, blue at pos2 is misplaced, and the present trio color (yellow at pos4 or white at pos5) is also misplaced relative to its guess index (though pos4/5 are beyond secret length, so only the first 4 matter). These constraints will help determine final positions.

Step 4: IDENTIFY IMPOSSIBLE COLORS - From this round, we can definitely rule out black because if black were present, pegs would be at most 4 (green 2 + red 1 + blue 1 = 4), contradicting pegs=5. Also, any color not in the guess is unaffected. Purple, orange, pink were already impossible. White or yellow cannot be individually eliminated yet.

Step 5: IDENTIFY UNKNOWNS - We know 3 of the 4 secret colors: green, red, blue. The fourth is either yellow or white. Positions are partially constrained: green cannot be at 0,2,3 (so must be at 1); red cannot be at 0,1 (so can be at 2/3); blue cannot be at 1,2 (so can be at 0/3). The present trio color's position is guided by round3: if yellow present, must be at pos0; if white present, must be at pos1. But pos1 is taken by green, so white cannot be present. This deduction merges across rounds. The unknown at this stage is whether the constraints definitively resolve the remaining positions.
analysis_result.analysis: Previous rounds: Round 2 established secret has 4 distinct colors from {red, blue, green, yellow, white, black}. Round 3 showed exactly one of {yellow, white, black} is present and locked in its guessed position. Round 4 pegs=5 forces green, red, blue, and one of {yellow, white} to be present, and rules out black. Combining all: green must be at position 1 (from its allowed positions), which conflicts with white requiring position 1, so white is impossible; thus yellow is the present trio color and must be at position 0 (from round3). Red and blue then occupy positions 2 and 3. With blue not allowed at position 2 (round4) and red allowed at both, we get blue at position 3 and red at position 2. Therefore, the secret is completely determined: [yellow, green, red, blue].
constraints: All positions are locked; no further guessing needed.
Pegs and positions from all rounds are consistent with this solution.
